cognitoMfaNotEnforced.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) do not enforce multi-factor authentication (MfaConfiguration is OFF or OPTIONAL). Without enforced MFA, credentials compromised via phishing or reuse are sufficient to sign in. Enforcing MFA is the single largest security lift Cognito offers.
cognitoMfaNotEnforced.shortDesc: Set MfaConfiguration=ON to require MFA on every sign-in

cognitoWeakPasswordPolicy.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have a weak password policy (MinimumLength < 12, or one or more of RequireUppercase / RequireLowercase / RequireNumbers / RequireSymbols is false). Weak password policies materially reduce the cost of credential-stuffing and brute-force attacks.
cognitoWeakPasswordPolicy.shortDesc: Require length >= 12 and all four character classes

cognitoAdvancedSecurityNotEnforced.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) do not have advanced security in ENFORCED mode (UserPoolAddOns.AdvancedSecurityMode is missing, OFF, or AUDIT). Advanced security features (adaptive authentication, compromised-credential detection) only apply enforcement actions when the mode is ENFORCED.

cognitoDeletionProtectionDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have DeletionProtection set to INACTIVE. A user pool can be deleted with a single API call in this state; the pool ID cannot be recreated and all users are lost.
cognitoDeletionProtectionDisabled.shortDesc: Enable DeletionProtection on every production user pool

cognitoNoEmailPhoneVerification.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) do not auto-verify email or phone (AutoVerifiedAttributes is empty). Without verification, an attacker can register an account claiming any email/phone and later use account-recovery flows on that unverified attribute.
cognitoNoEmailPhoneVerification.shortDesc: Auto-verify at least one of email / phone_number

cognitoNoLambdaTriggers.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have no Lambda triggers configured (LambdaConfig is empty). Lambda triggers such as PreAuthentication / PostAuthentication / PreTokenGeneration are the only way to add custom auth-time telemetry, risk scoring, or claim customisation.
cognitoNoLambdaTriggers.shortDesc: Wire at least one Lambda trigger for auth observability or custom logic

cognitoAccountRecoveryNotConfigured.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have no account-recovery mechanism configured (AccountRecoverySetting is missing or RecoveryMechanisms is empty). Users who lose credentials have no self-service reset path; every reset becomes a support ticket.
cognitoAccountRecoveryNotConfigured.shortDesc: Configure AccountRecoverySetting with at least one recovery mechanism

cognitoSingleRecoveryOption.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have exactly one account-recovery mechanism configured. If that channel (e.g. the user's only email address) becomes unavailable, self-service recovery is blocked. A second mechanism provides a fall-back.
cognitoSingleRecoveryOption.shortDesc: Configure at least two recovery mechanisms with distinct priorities

cognitoUnusedUserPool.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have zero users (EstimatedNumberOfUsers=0). Empty pools accumulate configuration drift and, on paid tiers (PLUS/ESSENTIALS), incur monthly fixed charges even with no active users.

cognitoTokenValidityTooLong.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with long-lived tokens (AccessTokenValidity > 60 min, IdTokenValidity > 60 min, or RefreshTokenValidity > 30 days). Long-lived tokens increase the window during which a stolen token remains valid.

cognitoDeviceTrackingDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have device tracking disabled (DeviceConfiguration is missing, or ChallengeRequiredOnNewDevice is false). Device tracking enables 'remembered-device' MFA suppression and detection of sign-ins from unknown devices.

cognitoResourcesWithoutTags.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have no tags applied. Tags enable cost allocation, tag-based IAM policies, and lifecycle governance.
cognitoResourcesWithoutTags.shortDesc: Tag every user pool (owner, environment, cost-centre)

cognitoCompromisedCredentialProtection.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have Advanced Security enabled but the CompromisedCredentialsRiskConfiguration event action is not set to BLOCK. Compromised-credentials detection is worthless if the response is just LOG or NO_ACTION — attackers can still complete sign-in with a leaked password.

cognitoSelfServiceSignUpNoVerification.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) allow public self-service sign-up (AdminCreateUserConfig.AllowAdminCreateUserOnly=false) with no email/phone verification required (AutoVerifiedAttributes is empty). Anyone can create an account with a made-up address, creating spam/abuse and fake-user risk.
cognitoSelfServiceSignUpNoVerification.shortDesc: Require email or phone verification when self-service sign-up is enabled

cognitoNoCustomDomain.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) use only the default amazoncognito.com domain with no custom domain configured. Custom domains give you a brandable auth URL and let you rotate the ACM cert independently; the default domain forces the AWS-owned certificate chain. Informational only.
cognitoNoCustomDomain.shortDesc: Configure a custom domain on user pools serving public traffic

cognitoNoWafAssociation.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have no WAFv2 WebACL associated. Without WAF, the hosted-UI sign-in / sign-up endpoints have no bot mitigation, no rate-based throttling, and no IP-reputation filtering — leaving them open to credential-stuffing and enumeration attacks.
cognitoNoWafAssociation.shortDesc: Associate a WAFv2 WebACL with the user pool

cognitoClientImplicitGrantEnabled.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with the Implicit OAuth grant enabled (AllowedOAuthFlows contains 'implicit'). The Implicit flow returns tokens in the URL fragment and is deprecated in OAuth 2.1 — Authorization Code + PKCE is the modern replacement.
cognitoClientImplicitGrantEnabled.shortDesc: Remove 'implicit' from AllowedOAuthFlows; use code + PKCE

cognitoClientTokenRevocationDisabled.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with EnableTokenRevocation=false. Stolen refresh tokens remain valid even after a user signs out — an attacker who captured a refresh token retains access until natural expiry.
cognitoClientTokenRevocationDisabled.shortDesc: Set EnableTokenRevocation=true on every app client

cognitoClientHttpCallbackUrl.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with an http:// CallbackURL (non-localhost). Auth codes and tokens are transmitted to the callback URL in the query string — over HTTP that traffic is exposed to any on-path attacker.
cognitoClientHttpCallbackUrl.shortDesc: Use https:// CallbackURLs for every app client

cognitoClientUserExistenceErrors.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with PreventUserExistenceErrors=LEGACY (or missing). LEGACY mode returns distinct errors for 'user not found' vs 'wrong password', enabling username enumeration by unauthenticated attackers.
cognitoClientUserExistenceErrors.shortDesc: Set PreventUserExistenceErrors=ENABLED on every app client

cognitoClientInsecureAuthFlow.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with ALLOW_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH in ExplicitAuthFlows. This flow sends the user's password in cleartext to Cognito (no SRP challenge); ALLOW_USER_SRP_AUTH is the secure alternative.

cognitoClientHttpLogoutUrl.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have at least one app client with an http:// LogoutURL (non-localhost). Sign-out redirects and any state carried in the URL are exposed to on-path attackers.
cognitoClientHttpLogoutUrl.shortDesc: Use https:// LogoutURLs for every app client

cognitoAccountTakeoverProtectionWeak.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have Advanced Security ENFORCED but the AccountTakeoverRiskConfiguration.Actions.HighAction.EventAction is not BLOCK (e.g., NO_ACTION or MFA_IF_CONFIGURED without enforcement). Detecting high-risk sign-ins without blocking them is close to no protection at all.

cognitoNoLoggingConfiguration.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have no LogDeliveryConfiguration set. Authentication events, admin actions, and user-management operations are not delivered to CloudWatch Logs / S3 / Firehose — no audit trail.
cognitoNoLoggingConfiguration.shortDesc: Configure LogDelivery to CloudWatch Logs (or S3/Firehose)

cognitoThreatProtectionAuditOnly.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have Advanced Security in AUDIT mode. Threats are detected and logged but no protective action is taken. Distinct from cognitoAdvancedSecurityNotEnforced (which flags OFF) — AUDIT is an intentional halfway step that many teams forget to graduate from.
cognitoThreatProtectionAuditOnly.shortDesc: Move AdvancedSecurityMode from AUDIT to ENFORCED

cognitoDefaultEmailSender.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) use EmailSendingAccount=COGNITO_DEFAULT. This limits to 50 emails/day, uses the AWS-owned no-reply@verificationemail.com sender (which many mail providers flag as phishing), and offers no bounce/complaint tracking. Production pools should use DEVELOPER (SES).

cognitoIdpHttpMetadataUrl.^description: {$COUNT} Cognito user pool(s) have an identity provider (SAML/OIDC) whose MetadataURL uses http://. SAML metadata / OIDC discovery documents fetched over HTTP can be replaced by an attacker who controls the network path — enabling federation-level impersonation.
cognitoIdpHttpMetadataUrl.shortDesc: Use https:// for identity provider MetadataURL
